title: Priority Specimen Prep Protocol
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"13f9o","text":"This protocol is designed to allow for fast and efficient processing of your samples once they arrive at the lab. For those who follow this protocol, it should take the processing time from your samples from many months, down to a few weeks (or even on the order of days in some cases). ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"8uhk2","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"19vcp","text":"Specimens can be done this way fresh or dried. They can also be sampled in the field - just take a picture of the tube barcode to include with your iNaturalist observation, and scan them in directly from the picture during iNat upload. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"d9v41","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"cf2f7","text":"The overall goal of this protocol is to significantly expedite the turnaround time at the lab, and to allow power users the opportunity to reduce the total number of specimens they need to collect, dry, organize, and mail. Specimens would no longer need to be universally collected, particularly for species they know they have collected and barcoded in the past, but where DNA can still help to confirm the species concept. Specimens would just be saved for the 10-20% of really interesting things, eliminating 80-90% of the specimen drying, organization, and shipping. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Prepare your materials: 5mL vials with dual barcodes ( CryoKING Multi-Coded Cryogenic Vials 5.0 ml ) Toothpicks Shotglass with water Handheld 2D scanner ( Example )

Open the tube, dip the toothpick in the water in the shot glass, and poke, roll, and/or scrape the toothpick on the specimen. For most specimens, such as agarics, boletes, polypores, and others, jab the end of the toothpick into the specimen four or five times: For powdery specimens like a puffball, just rolling the toothpick on the surface will deposit cells around the exterior of the toothpick: For resupinate specimens or in other various cases, simply scraping the surface of the specimen a few times will get enough cells onto the toothpick:
Scan the barcode directly into the iNaturalist/Mushroom Observer observation. "Tube Number" field on iNaturalist.
Mail in the tubes along with the specimens. They can be placed back in the original bags, in any order, all facing the same way.
creator: Stephen D Douglas Russell
